data.title: 4 Upcoming Dividend Increases
data.description:
The market volatility has died down in recent weeks, with a large win streak for the S&P. As always, I focus on dividend increases by some of the best companies in the market. It's too hard to try to predict what the macro picture will look like tomorrow, let alone a year from now. I watch dividend increases closely, especially by quality companies that can keep that performance year in and year out.

This week, I have four more increases to highlight, and I'll present more information about the companies and the methodology behind the selection.

Companies that consistently increase their dividends are often considered better investments over time, indicating strong financial health and stability. Regular dividend hikes suggest that a company is producing strong cash flow and can provide both income and total returns for investors.

My investment approach centers around buying, holding, and adding to positions in companies that both pay increasing dividends and can also beat a relevant benchmark (better than average returns). I've gathered this information as part of my investment strategy and am excited to share my findings with my Seeking Alpha audience. This list is designed to help you make informed investment choices.

How I Created The Lists

The information presented here is the result from combining two data sources: the “U.S. Dividend Champions” spreadsheet and upcoming dividend data from NASDAQ. This combination meshes data on companies with a track record of consistent dividend growth and the timeliness of their dividend increases. It's important to note that every company in this list has at least 5 years of dividend growth history.

To be considered for this list, a company must offer higher total annual dividends. Although a company might not increase its dividend every calendar year, its overall annual dividend can still grow.

What Is The Ex-Dividend Date?

The ex-dividend date is when you must own shares to qualify for an upcoming dividend or distribution. To be eligible, you must have bought the shares by the end of the preceding business day. For instance, if the ex-dividend date is Tuesday, you must have acquired the shares by the market close on Monday. If the ex-dividend date falls on a Monday (or a Tuesday following a holiday on Monday), you must have purchased the shares by the previous Friday.

Dividend Streak Categories

Here are the definitions of the streak categories, as I'll use them throughout the piece.

  • King: 50+ years
  • Champion/Aristocrat: 25+ years
  • Contender: 10–24 years
  • Challenger: 5+ years
Category Count
King 0
Champion 0
Contender 3
Challenger 1

The Dividend Increases List

The data is sorted by the ex-dividend date (ascending) and then by the streak (descending):

Name Ticker Streak Forward Yield Ex-Div Date Increase Percent Streak Category
Paychex, Inc. (PAYX) 15 2.87 12-May-25 10.20% Contender
Apple Inc. (AAPL) 13 0.53 12-May-25 4.00% Contender
Winmark Corporation (WINA) 5 1.03 14-May-25 6.67% Challenger
Pool Corporation (POOL) 15 1.69 15-May-25 4.17% Contender

Field Definitions

Streak: Years of dividend growth history are sourced from the U.S. Dividend Champions spreadsheet.

Forward Yield: The payout rate is calculated by dividing the new payout rate by the current share price.

Ex-Dividend Date: This is the date you need to own the stock.

Increase Percent: The percent increase.

Streak Category: This is the company's overall dividend history classification.

Show Me The Money

Here is a table that shows the new and old rates and the percentage increase. The table is sorted by ex-dividend day in ascending order and dividend streak in descending order.

Ticker Old Rate New Rate Increase Percent
PAYX 0.98 1.08 10.20%
AAPL 0.25 0.26 4.00%
WINA 0.9 0.96 6.67%
POOL 1.2 1.25 4.17%

Additional Metrics

Some different metrics related to these companies include yearly pricing action and the P/E ratio. The table is sorted the same way as the table above.

Ticker Current Price 52 Week Low 52 Week High PE Ratio % Off Low % Off High
PAYX 150.32 112.98 158.37 32.29 33% Off Low 5% Off High
AAPL 196.56 169.21 259.81 37.78 15% Off Low 25% Off High
WINA 374.21 295.79 429 23.62 27% Off Low 13% Off High
POOL 295.8 284.27 394.22 41.67 4% Off Low 25% Off High

Tickers By Yield And Growth Rates

I've organized the table in descending order, allowing investors to prioritize the current yield. Additionally, the table includes some historical dividend growth rates. I've also incorporated the “Chowder Rule,” the sum of the current yield and the five-year dividend growth rate.

Ticker Yield 1 Yr DG 3 Yr DG 5 Yr DG 10 Yr DG Chowder Rule
PAYX 2.87 10.1 14.1 9.6 10.2 12.2
POOL 1.69 9.1 14.5 16.9 18.5 18.5
WINA 1.03 12.5 26 29.2 31.1 30.2
AAPL 0.53 4.2 4.4 5.4 7.9 5.9

Historical Returns

My investment strategy focuses on identifying stocks that consistently outperform the market and enhance their dividend payouts. I rely on two primary ETF benchmarks as comparison points versus individual companies. I will stop my research if a potential company cannot beat the benchmark ETF.

For broad exposure to U.S. equity markets, excluding the REIT sector, I recommend the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). SCHD boasts a strong performance record, offers a yield that exceeds that of the S&P 500, and consistently delivers increasing dividends.

The ten-year dividend growth rate is one of the four main factors in the index that SCHD tracks. Since it's a key factor, I'll exclude companies without one from the following comparisons.

Based on this analysis (and further due diligence), I have included several companies in my portfolio and will sometimes make timely purchases of existing holdings. The charts below assume dividends are reinvested.

Here's everyone compared against SCHD.

As the reference point, SCHD returned 169% over the past decade. I had a suspicion, because I follow these companies, but all of the companies this week managed to beat SCHD over the past decade.

Apple was the top dog, which shouldn't be a huge surprise. They've been an absolute cashflow monster with a generous capital return program for many years.

POOL finished in second, but it's had an interesting journey. It was a strong company before COVID, as you can see, its pink line was the top-performing stock for a number of years. The thing was, it got so overvalued and overheated in the post-COVID stock frenzy. Since peaking at the end of 2021, it had been range-bound for a few years before finally deflating more recently. It's still a solid company and could be worth a closer look here.

WINA finished third, though their total return of 390% is very close to POOL's 404%. It's pretty subjective with a ten-year horizon, moving it back and forth a few days for comparison sake may make them switch position. WINA generally beat SCHD, but by a narrower amount. You can see its lime green performance over time, offering a few extra percentage points over SCHD. In the middle of 2022, they got bid up to current levels where they've been range-bound as well.

Finally, PAYX offered a solid 314% total return with little extra fanfare. The company was a routine SCHD-beater, generally offering a 2-3% additional return each year. They also did not experience any of the choppiness of seeing rapid price appreciation, as some of the companies experienced. Slow and steady has proven once again to be a boring but solid investment strategy.

Next Steps

I maintain high standards for the stocks in my portfolio. I want to hold companies that offer significant alpha over SCHD, not just slightly beating it. These articles are my opportunity to see what has done well and review my current holdings as they provide their annual increases.

I own AAPL already, and I actually recently reorganized my dividend growth portfolio and removed WINA and POOL. They are still interesting companies to me; I was spreading myself too thin with over 30 individual holdings. For management purposes, I trimmed it back up to 25 holdings. At a certain point, you have to ask yourself, is this 26th, 27th, 30th, etc, idea of mine as good as the first few I have? They are all on my watchlist, but short of committing to a full ETF of my own (which I would argue 25 holdings will do), and also allocating exclusively to my dividend growth portfolio, it becomes a bit unwieldy.

At this point, I don't have plans to review them or PAYX any further and will continue reinvesting my AAPL dividends.

I’d love to hear your thoughts on my strategy, so feel free to share yours in the comments below! As always, do your due diligence before making any investment decisions.

data.title: Meta, Please Kill Reality Labs
data.description:
Meta (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:META) has underperformed the market since we last recommended against investing in it. The company has hit cumulative losses of more than $60 billion for Reality Labs since late 2020, and now thanks to artificial intelligence, the company's capex is expected to double from 2023 to 2025.

Despite the company's inability to "right-size" its bets, which we'll discuss in more detail below, the incredible strength of its core business and the strong cash flow there make the company a valuable investment.

Meta Revenue

The company managed to grow its revenue signifcantly year-over-year, a substantial improvement given its size.

The company managed to generate more than $42 billion in revenue in the quarter, the vast majority from the US and Canada. However, all of the company's segments and countries outperformed year-over-year, there's no standout region that's underperforming substantially. That revenue improvement is still virtually entirely through the company's ad business.

However, it's key to the company's ability to drive shareholder returns on a $1.5 trillion valuation.

Meta Segment Results

As seen here, the company's Realty Labs continues to generate minimal revenue, with a strong QoQ and YoY decline, and TTM revenue of $2.1 billion, a mere 11% of the division's TTM losses.

Specifically, the company's advertising revenue saw almost 20% growth from $36 billion to more than $41 billion YoY. Outside of the last quarter, which is traditionally the company's strongest, the company had record strong revenue. 1Q 2024 also represented substantial strength over 1Q 2023 as the company showed a trend of improvements.

The company's Realty Labs actually saw revenue decline YoY though as the segment has continued to struggle to gather any meaningful traction. More importantly, losses in the segment expanded YoY to more than $4.2 billion despite the company's goal to manage its expenditures. The division has cost the company $18.1 billion in operating losses over the last 12 months.

That's 20% of the company's operating income, a massive loss on a "bet." The company has maintained a strong operating margin as a result of success in its advertising business along with manageable fixed costs.

Meta Expenses

The company has continued to manage its expenditures while investing heavily in R&D.

The company maintaining fixed or improving margins is key to the company's ability to continue driving shareholder returns. Specifically, despite the company's massive Realty Labs spending, it needs to show a consistent ability to manage its expenditures across the board. The company has reduced its expenses, but it still needs to grow profits more to generate returns.

Meta Capital Expenditures

As a part of this, the company has spent massive amounts of growing cash on capital expenditures.

The company expects 2025 capex to potentially hit $70 billion, a massive jump over 2024, which was already a massive jump over 2023. The company's capex is now well over double where it was in 2023 as the company continues to chase its goals across artificial intelligence. However, the company is continuing to open source its AI, which could hurt future profits.

Take other groundbreaking technologies, such as Windows (closed source) or Linux (open source), and the revenue differences. This isn't to say that there's no opportunity to make money off of open-source technology, but when you take a stance to not charge for the technology you're spending tens of billions on, you owe investors a clear path to justify that investment.

Meta has not presented one.

Meta Growth

However, Meta is continuing to see consistent growth across its portfolio of assets.

The company's daily active users grew from 3.24 billion to 3.43 billion. That's high single-digit growth as the company has continued to see substantial popularity across what's an impressive portfolio of assets. It's also substantial because the company now has almost half of the population globally as daily users on its apps.

That shows the scale of the company's operations as it continues to grow. But it also shows the company's limitations. It won't be able to triple in size from daily active users because there's not enough people on the planet.

The company has done a fantastic job of earning more from the same customers. The company has managed to deliver mid-single-digit growth in ad impressions to the company's customers. Continuing to grow this number along with substantial growth from Q1'23 to Q1'24 is key to the company continuing its revenue growth.

At the same time, the company has managed to increase its price per impression by double digits from a worldwide perspective. This is with key performance in some markets like the US and Canada. That growth is essential along with the company's impressions to growing overall revenue, and the company has managed to tick it up in recent quarters.

That shows substantial demand in the market for the company's offerings and it's key to the company driving overall shareholder returns.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is Meta's lack of ability to "right-size" its bets. Take Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) for example. Google's "Other Bets" incorporates bets like Waymo, Wing, Google Fiber, etc., plenty of unique and interesting ideas. The "Other Bets" division at Google earns ~$450 million in quarterly revenue, similar to what Realty Labs is earning.

However, operating income losses from the division are just more than $1 billion, or 25% of what Meta is losing on Reality Labs alone. A new idea or investment isn't just dependent on how much it earns, it's dependent on how much the company needs to invest and the return on invested capital. Meta's over-investment (i.e. non right-sized bets) in ideas that haven't proven their ability to generate sufficient returns are a substantial risk to long-term shareholder returns.

Conclusion

Meta is a company we've soured on before as the company continues to spend tens of billions on new initiatives with questionable return potential. Whether that's Reality Labs or artificial intelligence, the company has surpassed $60 billion in Reality Labs spending and experts question whether the company will ever earn a return.

The company, however, does have an incredibly strong core business. The company is growing customers, ad impressions per customers, and ad profits per impression. That's a triple whammy that's leading to growing cash flow for the company, making it a valuable long-term investment.
